f4e425ff-b414-4238-8198-ac7478fe7c3a.classification_raw:
1. Shoulder blades & upper back
2. Head & neck
3. Grip & wrists
4. Elbows position
5. Upper arms (forearm angle to elbow)
6. Chest & ribcage (rib position)
7. Core bracing
8. Hips & glutes contact with bench
9. Feet & base stability
10. Bar path (movement line over chest)
11. Lower-body position (leg drive)
f4e425ff-b414-4238-8198-ac7478fe7c3a.response:
What I’m seeing: you’re doing a flat dumbbell bench press on a flat bench with heavy dumbbells. The camera angle is actually pretty solid for judging your upper body. Across the reps, your range of motion looks decent, you’re getting the bells down near chest level, and you’re controlling them rather than just bouncing. I also notice some left-right unevenness: your left arm looks a little more flared and slightly ahead/behind the right at different points, and the dumbbells don’t track perfectly symmetrically. Your lower body looks loose—legs spread wide, but I can’t see a strong planted-foot setup creating much leg drive into the bench.

## What looks good
- You’re moving the dumbbells through a solid ROM and getting a good stretch at the bottom.
- The reps look controlled overall, especially considering the load. You’re not letting the weights crash around.

## Main fixes
- **Create a tighter upper-back setup.** Before you start, pinch your shoulder blades together and keep your upper back planted on the bench. In these frames, your pressing looks a bit “loose” through the shoulders, which can shift stress away from the chest and into the shoulders.
- **Build a stronger base with your feet.** Your lower body doesn’t look locked in. Set your feet firmly on the floor and keep them there the whole set. You want your feet flat if possible, with pressure through the floor so your core and torso stay tight. A stable lower body gives you a better platform to press from.
- **Keep hips down, but use leg drive.** Stay glued to the bench with your butt in contact, but push through the floor to reinforce your arch and keep your chest high.
- **Clean up the arm path.** Your elbows appear a bit inconsistent rep to rep, especially on the left side. Try to keep the same groove every rep so the dumbbells rise and lower evenly.
- **Pack the shoulders if they start to drift.** If you feel your shoulders coming forward or your back flattening, pause and reset. Once the shoulders lose their packed position, pressing power usually drops and the movement gets more shoulder-dominant.
- **Consider slightly reducing load if this asymmetry keeps showing up.** The weight is manageable, but if form changes and uneven lockout keep happening, backing off a little and rebuilding with cleaner reps will help more long term.

## Mental cues
- “Shoulder blades pinned.”
- “Chest high.”
- “Feet planted, push the floor.”
- “Same path every rep.”
- “Stay tight from feet to hands.”

Overall: strong effort and definitely not bad form—your biggest gains will come from getting tighter through the upper back and lower body so the press is more stable and symmetrical.

699488e0-f5db-4a68-9ef1-cc5800230143.classification_raw:
Bench press

1. Foot placement & base
2. Leg drive & stance width
3. Glute contact & hip position
4. Back arch & ribcage position
5. Shoulder blade position
6. Elbow position (angle) & tuck
7. Wrist alignment & grip
8. Bar path over chest
9. Head & neck position
10. Shoulder height (relative to bench)
699488e0-f5db-4a68-9ef1-cc5800230143.response:
What I observe: you’re doing an incline Smith machine press from a front angle. You complete multiple reps with a fairly consistent bar path, and the bar comes down to around upper chest/clavicle level. Your feet are spread wide, but your foot pressure looks inconsistent, and your upper body tightness seems to loosen a bit as the reps go on. Your elbows also look somewhat flared out, especially near the bottom.

## What looks good
- You’re getting decent range of motion and bringing the bar down to a consistent touch point around the upper chest.
- Rep tempo looks controlled overall — not dive-bombing the descent, which is good for safety and tension.

## Main fixes
- **Build a tighter base with your feet.** Right now your feet are wide, but they don’t look like they’re creating much leg drive. Set them back as far as you comfortably can while keeping them planted. Flat feet are usually best here so you can actually push through the floor. Think of your feet as part of the lift, not just somewhere to rest them.
- **Keep four points of contact the whole set:** head, upper back, glutes, and feet. From the images, your upper body position looks a little loose as reps continue. You want your upper back locked into the bench the entire time.
- **Retract and plant your shoulder blades harder.** Your chest could stay prouder and higher. Pinch the shoulder blades together and keep that upper back as your pressing platform.
- **Watch the elbow flare.** Your elbows appear a bit too far out to the sides at the bottom. Aim for roughly a **45-degree angle** relative to your torso rather than straight out. That usually puts the shoulders in a stronger, safer pressing position.
- **Brace before each rep.** Take a big breath into your diaphragm/gut and press with a rigid torso. As soon as your legs or core loosen up, reset. Don’t keep repping with a soft base.
- **Grip and upper-back tension:** squeeze the bar hard and think about “bending the bar” or “ripping it in half.” That can help keep your upper back engaged and improve elbow position.
- **Camera angle note:** this front angle is helpful for symmetry, but a 45-degree side angle would make it much easier to judge bar path, wrist stacking, touch point, and whether your forearms are vertical at the bottom.

## Mental cues
- **“Feet down, drive the floor.”**
- **“Chest up, upper back pinned.”**
- **“Elbows 45 degrees.”**
- **“Big breath, stay tight.”**
- **“Touch high chest, press back strong.”**

Overall: solid start, especially your control and consistency. Biggest win for you will be getting much tighter through the feet, core, and upper back so each rep starts from a more stable platform. If you want, I can also give you a **rep-by-rep breakdown** or help you adjust your **grip width** from these images.

7e4828df-5f9c-4cd0-a3bc-453fba272db5.exercise_label: how should I do incline bench press?
7e4828df-5f9c-4cd0-a3bc-453fba272db5.user_query: how should I do incline bench press?
7e4828df-5f9c-4cd0-a3bc-453fba272db5.response:
Use a grip just outside shoulder width and keep your shoulder blades pinched together with your chest up on the bench. As you lower the bar, think about “bending the bar into a U” to keep your elbows from flaring too much, then press up smoothly while keeping your upper back planted and feet driving into the floor.

One cue for your next set: **keep your forearms vertical at the bottom**.

If you want, I can also break down bench angle, elbow position, or common incline mistakes.

e21851c2-9a99-40d6-a3b2-4dd26a59b118.response:
You look solid overall. From these frames, your bar path looks fairly controlled and you’re touching in a consistent lower-chest/upper-ab area, which is good, but your wrists look a bit extended and your elbows may be drifting a little wide at the bottom.

Next set, try this one cue: **stack the bar directly over the heel of your palm and forearm the whole rep—squeeze the bar hard and keep wrists straighter.**

Also worth checking: keep your upper back pinned to the bench, use steadier leg drive, and a side-angle video would make bar path and elbow position much easier to assess.

388c49ae-6584-478f-bc8f-163714b3072c.classification_raw:
Bench press (barbell)

1. Feet & base
2. Glutes & hips on bench
3. Low back & lumbar position
4. Core brace & ribcage
5. Shoulder position (protraction/retraction)
6. Shoulder blade “set” (scapula)
7. Elbow position (angle) & forearm alignment
8. Hand/wrist position on bar
9. Head & neck position
10. Bar path relative to chest
11. Torso-to-bar distance and arch
12. End-range lockout position (elbows/shoulders)
388c49ae-6584-478f-bc8f-163714b3072c.response:
I’m seeing a flat barbell bench press from a front-left angle. You’ve got a noticeable arch, your upper back stays on the bench, your feet are planted, and the bar is touching around the lower chest/upper sternum area. Across reps, the bar path looks fairly consistent, but I also notice your elbows flare a bit as you press, your wrists look somewhat bent back under the bar, and your foot position looks wide and a little loose for maximizing leg drive. Also, with this weight and no visible spotter, safety is worth mentioning.

## What looks good
- **You’re touching in a solid spot on the chest.** The bar comes down to a good lower-chest/upper sternum area rather than drifting too high toward the neck.
- **You’ve got a decent base already.** Your upper back appears planted and you maintain an arch instead of going completely flat and loose on the bench.

## Main fixes
- **Tuck the elbows a bit more on the way down.** Right now they look a little too flared at parts of the press. A better position is roughly **45 degrees relative to your torso**. Think about **“bending the bar into a U”** or **“protect your armpits.”** That should help engage your lats, improve shoulder comfort, and make the press more efficient.
- **Create more upper-back tightness before the unrack.** I’d like to see you really **squeeze your shoulder blades together** and keep them pinned throughout the set. Your upper back should feel like a solid platform you’re pressing from.
- **Improve leg drive setup.** Your feet are planted, but they look pretty wide and not especially tucked back. Try bringing your feet **farther back as much as you comfortably can**, while keeping your **glutes on the bench**. That’ll let you drive more force through the floor and stay tighter through the whole set.
- **Stack the wrists more directly under the bar.** Your wrists appear extended backward. Try to hold the bar so it sits **more directly over the wrist joint**, not back toward the fingers. That’ll improve force transfer and reduce wrist strain.
- **Grip the bar harder and use the bar-bending cue.** Actively trying to **“rip the bar in half”** can help keep your upper back engaged and naturally guide your elbows into a safer pressing position.
- **Safety note:** you don’t appear to have a spotter. For a working set like this, especially if you’re close to failure, having a **spotter** or using safeties would be a very good idea.

## Mental cues
- **Squeeze shoulder blades into the bench**
- **Big breath, brace hard**
- **Bend the bar into a U**
- **Elbows at 45 degrees**
- **Drive through the feet**
- **Press from a tight upper back**

Overall: **solid base, definitely not bad**, and you’re already doing a few important things right. Your biggest gains will come from **more upper-back tightness, better wrist stacking, and slightly less elbow flare**. If you want, I can also give you a **rep-by-rep breakdown** or a **3-step bench setup checklist** based on this video.
